title: Mesh construction styles — box / smooth / chunked as recipe-selectable variants
intent: The shipped generator (and the per-part `headShape` / `torsoShape` / `limbShape` knobs added in GDP-2026-05-21-002) let an individual recipe pick PrimitiveShape per body part. That's enough for one bomber. But the FAMILY-level identity asks a higher-level question: 'what KIND of bomber is this?' — a blocky toy-robot, a smooth toy-creature, or a stacked-cube voxel-thing. This story adds a `recipe.bodyStyle` enum that picks a coordinated set of per-part shape defaults, plus a generator dispatch that knows three distinct construction modes. The styles share the same joint hierarchy + the same palette channels — only the underlying geometry construction differs.
rationale: Source: user request 2026-05-21 — 'различные типы генерации меша' (different mesh construction modes). The visual-style.md doc settled the 'toy-scale industrial diorama' aesthetic but didn't lock the construction primitive. Box-only is the v0 baseline; smooth (capsule-and-sphere) tests the engine's path for non-axis-aligned geometries and produces a softer, less-mechanical look; chunked (multi-box-per-part voxel-stack) tests batching and gives a 'pixel-3D' aesthetic. Marked `could` because the gameplay difference is zero — this is pure visual exploration. If sprint capacity is tight, dev picks ONE style as production and parks the others as bench-only options. If capacity allows all three, they ship as recipe-selectable.
acceptanceHints: Depends on GDP-2026-05-21-001 (each style produces the same multi-mesh tree shape; only the per-mesh BufferGeometry changes).

Recipe surface: `recipe.bodyStyle: 'box' | 'smooth' | 'chunked'` (default 'box'). When set, it overrides the per-part style enums from GDP-2026-05-21-002 (the per-part overrides become trailing-edge fine-tuning).
STYLE 1 — `box` (default, ships with S101):
Each part is a single BoxGeometry. Hard panel-seam-friendly, mechanical look. The v0 + S101 generator already does this; this story keeps it as the default.
STYLE 2 — `smooth`:
Each part uses a softer primitive:
torso       → CapsuleGeometry (height = torsoHeight, radius = torsoWidth/2)
head        → SphereGeometry (radius = headSize/2, segments 12×8)
upperArm/forearm/upperLeg/lowerLeg → CapsuleGeometry (height = limbLength/2, radius = limbWidth/2)
hand/foot   → SphereGeometry (radius = limbWidth × 0.7)

Result: rounded silhouette, 'toy-creature' read, no visible panel seams. Panel-seam vertex-colour trick from GDP-2026-05-21-004 still applies but is barely visible on rounded geometry — that's by design, smooth bombers don't read as industrial.
STYLE 3 — `chunked`:

Each part is replaced by a 2×N×2 stack of small BoxGeometries fused into one BufferGeometry. Example: an upperArm in box mode is one box; in chunked mode it's 2×4×2 = 16 small boxes stacked along the limb axis.
Result: 'pixel-3D' / 'voxel-stack' aesthetic — visible chunky steps along every limb, more vertex-colour real estate, plays well with stripe patterns from GDP-2026-05-21-004.

Each chunked part is still a SINGLE Mesh (one merged BufferGeometry for all the sub-boxes) so the joint hierarchy stays at 10 meshes per bomber. The chunks live inside the part, not as separate scene nodes.
Configurable chunk granularity per recipe: `recipe.bodyStyle === 'chunked'` enables `recipe.chunkResolution: 1..4` (default 2) — how many chunks along each axis of each part.

Generator dispatch: a top-level `generateBomberMeshTree(recipe)` reads `recipe.bodyStyle` and routes each part-build through one of three builder functions. The per-style builders share the limb-pivot hierarchy + the palette painting path; only `geometryForPart(partName, partSize, palette, style, chunkResolution)` differs.

Style + accessory compatibility: all 5 accessories from GDP-2026-05-21-005 work with all 3 styles. The accessory builders pick their own primitives (antennae stays a cylinder regardless of body style) — that's intentional, accessories preserve their identity across styles.

Style + texturing compatibility: all 3 texturing layers from GDP-2026-05-21-004 work with all 3 styles. Panel seams are most visible on box, decals work equally on all three, stripe / spot patterns work on all three but read differently (boxes get crisp stripes, smooth gets soft stripes, chunked gets stair-stepped stripes — all intentional).

Bench UI: a new top-level dropdown 'Body style' with 3 options + a `chunkResolution` slider that only appears when style=chunked. Switching style triggers a full rebuild (not incremental — the whole tree's geometries change).
Determinism: same seed + same style + same chunkResolution → bit-identical mesh tree. Test it across all 3 styles.

Performance: smooth uses ~3× more vertices per part than box (sphere vs box). At 10 meshes per bomber + 4 bombers, smooth costs ~12× the vertex count of box (~4000 → ~12000 vertices per bomber × 4 = 48000 total). Within budget per visual-style.md §11. Chunked at resolution 2 costs ~8× box (each part is 8 sub-boxes); at resolution 4 costs ~64× — visible perf hit, document it as 'use only on the bench for screenshots, not in real arenas'.

Production choice: until the user picks one style as the Kaboom Crew production look (GDP-2026-05-21-007 integrates it), all 3 stay available via recipe. The bench README documents which style each family looks best in (e.g. 'mint' family looks better smooth, 'slate' family looks better box).

Open question (documented in characters-and-visual.md after this lands): does the production Kaboom Crew gameplay arena allow recipe-driven body styles (so two players can have different styles in the same round), or does the arena lock all bombers to one style? Default recommendation: arena allows per-player styles — fully player-driven identity beats forced uniformity.

Out of scope (explicit): more styles (lowpoly-mesh, voxel-with-bevels, sphereharmonic, etc.). Three is the design budget; more is exploration-not-production. Per-part style mixing within a single bomber (head=smooth, torso=chunked) — also out, ugly under top-down lighting, deferred indefinitely.
